title: Understand: The Battle Over Ratification and the Importance of Compromise
description: Check your understanding of the battle over ratification and the importance of compromise in this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: The Road to Reform, the Constitutional Convention, and the Battle for Ratification
The Battle Over Ratification and the Importance of Compromise
Understand: The Battle Over Ratification and the Importance of Compromise

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was needed for the Constitution to become the law of the land?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: At least nine states had to ratify it.
All of the delegates had to sign it on September 17, 1787.
The president had to agree to serve only two terms.
Countries in Europe, especially Britain and France, had to recognize it.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of these statements about the Constitutional Convention is true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: All of the states except Delaware sent delegates to the Convention.
Newspapers filed weekly reports that kept people aware of the debates.
Fewer than half of the delegates signed the Constitution at the Convention.
The delegates’ discussions were kept secret from the public.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Those who were opposed to the ratification of the Constitution were known as which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Loyalists
Federalists
Anti-Federalists
Tories

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the first state to ratify the Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Pennsylvania
Delaware
Virginia
North Carolina

assessment_items.question_content:
**In 1789, James Madison gave a speech in support of adding amendments to the Constitution. This is particularly significant because Madison\_\_\_\_.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: had always supported a bill of rights
had initially been reluctant to support a bill of rights
had joined the dissenters in refusing to sign the Constitution
was the oldest delegate at the Convention

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which famous founder, living in France at the time of the Constitutional Convention, helped convince James Madison of the value of a bill of rights?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Samuel Adams
Patrick Henry
John Adams
Thomas Jefferson

assessment_items.question_content:
**The Constitutional Convention established which type of legislature for the U.S. government?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Bicameral
Unicameral
Diametric
Bipartisan

assessment_items.question_content:
**During the Constitutional Convention, James Wilson argued that the executive branch should be led by \_\_\_\_.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a powerful king
a single president
a multi-member executive council
a figurehead with no real executive authority

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of these people is considered a Federalist?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: James Wilson
Gouverneur Morris
George Washington
Elbridge Gerry

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the main argument in Brutus No.1?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The United States is too large to survive as a republic under the Constitution.
The Constitution does not need a Bill of Rights to protect individual liberties.
All states should have the same number of representatives in the legislature.
The Articles of Confederation need to be replaced by the Constitution to create a strong central government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was one argument that Mercy Otis Warren included in her *Observations*?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The United States does not need a standing national army as it could be used to suppress opposition to the government.
The United States should return to British rule, as rebellions will continue under the new Constitution.
The delegates should ratify the Constitution because it creates a more stable government than the Articles of Confederation.
The Constitution should be amended because the United States needs a more powerful national government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What does it mean that the Constitution needed ratification?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The delegates at the Constitutional Convention needed to sign their names to the document, showing their support.
The American Congress needed to approve the new Constitution by a majority vote to replace the Articles of Confederation.
The American people needed to approve the new form of government for it to be legitimate.
The states needed to implement the principles of the Constitution by adding certain parts to their state constitutions.
